notes: Hazardous waste overrides all other logic.
If the item is not yours, return to owner.
Sensitive personal information must be destroyed or securely wiped.
If the item is a hazard to pets, kids, or people, it must be removed immediately.
Unsafe damage triggers hazardous waste or safe disposal.
Expired, spoiled, or past-safe-use items must be discarded.
Visibly dirty, moldy, stained, or unhygienic items should be discarded.
If the item is not safe, sanitary, or in good enough condition to keep, discard.
If the item is in a condition where you don't want to use it, release it.
If you wouldn't give it to someone you care about, it shouldn't stay.
Sharp edges, frayed cords, or safety risks require hazardous waste or safe disposal.
If the item is so worn out you avoid using it, discard it.
If you cannot use the product before expiration/end-of-life, release it.
Living things that are 'just surviving' should be ethically rehomed.
Official documents that must be legally retained should be kept or digitized + kept.
Items donation centers do not accept should be trashed or recycled.
If throwing the item in regular trash is unsafe, illegal, or harmful, recycle or dispose properly.
If the item cannot realistically be used or wanted by a donation center, do not donate.
If you cannot or do not want to keep caring for something, rehome it responsibly.
Guilt-based keeping triggers journaling/processing.
Avoidance of feelings indicates emotional processing needed.
Fear, anxiety, or emotional discomfort signals a need for reflection.
Sentimental meaning triggers journaling unless digitization is possible.
If meaning can be preserved digitally, digitize then release.
Multiple items representing the same memory triggers consolidation reflection.
Item tied to an outdated or aspirational identity requires emotional processing.
If you wouldn't notice it missing AND nothing important would change, emotional detour recommended.
Lack of joy triggers reflection before practical logic.
Future misalignment triggers journaling to clarify identity and direction.
Regular or predictable use indicates the item should be kept.
If the item is not genuinely useful, release it.
If the item does not serve a meaningful purpose right now, release it unless sentimental logic applies.
If the item has not been used or added value in the past year, release it unless future use is high.
If there is no realistic use in the next 3 or 12 months, release it.
If you do not want the item, release it unless it is necessary or sentimental.
If you do not need the item, release it unless it is regularly used or emotionally meaningful.
If you already own something that does the same job better, release this item.
If this is not the item you reach for when you need its function, release it.
If you avoid using the item because it is inconvenient or high-effort, release it.
If the item is not easy to use, release it.
If the item is uncomfortable or unpleasant to use, release it.
If the item does not work as intended, repair or upcycle it.
If the item is missing parts or accessories, repair or complete it.
Duplicates trigger a reflection path to choose the best and release the rest.
If you would not buy this again today, release it.
If a digital version exists that serves the same purpose, digitize then release.
If the item is not worth packing, moving, and unpacking, release it.
If the value does not justify the space it occupies, release it.
If you do not have a clear, specific place to store this in your home, release it.
If the item will not fit comfortably without crowding or overflow, release it.
If keeping this forces something else you value to lose space, release it.
If the item tends to migrate into clutter piles, release it.
If the item is awkwardly shaped or bulky in a way that makes storage difficult, release it.
If the item is too heavy or large to move reasonably, release it.
If the item is not suited to the climate, lifestyle, or space of your home, release it.
If the item requires special storage you cannot realistically maintain, release it.
If the item can be digitized, downsized, or simplified, digitize then release.
If the item is not worth the time, space, or energy it demands, release it.
If you would not carry this item for 3 miles in the rain, it is not worth keeping.
If you would not grab this in a fire, it is not a priority item.
If the item requires maintenance and you are not willing to do it, release it.
If the item creates more chores than it saves, release it.
If keeping the item adds to your mental load, release it.
Project stagnation triggers reflection or journaling.
Avoidance loops indicate emotional or logistical friction; reflection recommended.
If the item’s life can be responsibly extended, repair or upcycle it.
If the item can be repurposed or upcycled, choose transformation over disposal.
Fallback rule: if no other rule triggers, keep the item.
